questions.text: Should Canada maintain a fully public healthcare system or allow private competition?
questions.subtext: Canadian healthcare wait times are among the worst in the OECD. Private clinics are expanding in BC, Alberta, and Quebec.

options.text: Fully public system — ban private parallel insurance and for-profit delivery entirely.
Mostly public — allow limited private clinics for overflow, but no private insurance.
Mixed system — allow private insurance and clinics to compete alongside the public system.
Market-led system — government funds a safety net only; most care is private.

questions.text: How should Canada tax high incomes and corporate profits?
questions.subtext: Canada's top federal rate is 33%. Some propose a wealth tax; others argue high taxes drive away investment.

options.text: Significantly raise corporate and top personal tax rates; implement a wealth tax on assets over $10M.
Close loopholes and raise rates modestly; make the system more progressive.
Keep current rates — the balance is roughly right.
Cut corporate taxes and flatten personal rates to attract investment and growth.

questions.text: What role should unions play in Canada's economy?
questions.subtext: Private sector union density has fallen from 35% to 15% since the 1980s. Gig work is growing. Wages have stagnated.

options.text: Expand union rights significantly — card-check, sectoral bargaining, ban replacement workers.
Strengthen existing union protections; make it easier to organize without going full sectoral.
Current framework is adequate — unions have enough power already.
Right-to-work reforms — reduce union power; workers should negotiate individually.

questions.text: What is the primary solution to Canada's housing affordability crisis?
questions.subtext: Home prices have doubled in a decade. Rents are at record highs. The Carney government faces intense pressure.

options.text: Massive public and non-profit housing construction; strict rent control; heavy speculation taxes.
Significant public investment plus zoning reform to increase supply; moderate rent controls.
Deregulate zoning and reduce development charges — let the market build.
Get government out of the housing market entirely — subsidies and rent control make it worse.

questions.text: How should Canada manage its federal deficit?
questions.subtext: Federal debt servicing costs $50B+ annually. Canada's debt-to-GDP ratio is lower than the G7 average but rising.

options.text: Invest heavily in public services and infrastructure — debt is sustainable and the economy needs stimulus.
Spend on priorities but plan to balance the budget over the medium term.
Cut non-essential spending to reduce the deficit meaningfully within this Parliament.
Urgent austerity — the deficit is a crisis requiring immediate, significant cuts.

questions.text: Should Canada privatize Crown corporations and public utilities?
questions.subtext: Canada Post, CBC, Via Rail, and provincial hydro companies are debated. Privatization has occurred in telecoms and airlines.

options.text: Expand public ownership — nationalize key sectors like telecoms, internet, and energy.
Keep current public corporations but reform them for efficiency.
Privatize CBC and Canada Post; retain hydro and rail for practical reasons.
Broad privatization — the private sector delivers services better and shouldn't be subsidized.

questions.text: Should Canada implement a universal basic income (UBI)?
questions.subtext: Ontario ran a pilot in 2017 (cancelled by Ford). Finland and several US cities have tested UBI. AI automation threatens jobs.

options.text: Yes — a meaningful UBI replacing the patchwork of welfare programs and topping up low incomes.
Pilot it more broadly — the evidence is promising but we need better data at scale.
No — targeted benefits are more efficient; UBI is too costly and crowds out other programs.
Strongly against — UBI disincentivizes work and is fiscally irresponsible.

questions.text: Is a carbon price the right tool to reduce Canadian emissions?
questions.subtext: The carbon tax was central to the 2025 federal election. Carney's Liberals are retooling it; Poilievre campaigned to axe it entirely.

options.text: Carbon pricing is a good start — but we also need direct regulation, public investment, and corporate mandates.
A well-designed carbon price with full rebates is the most efficient mechanism.
Replace with technology subsidies and voluntary measures — carbon pricing hurts ordinary Canadians.
Abolish carbon pricing — economic growth and market innovation are the only real solutions.

questions.text: Should Canada's $10/day childcare program be expanded or scaled back?
questions.subtext: The national childcare deal costs ~$30B annually. Waitlists remain. Some argue it crowds out private operators.

options.text: Fully fund and expand — universal childcare is infrastructure as essential as roads.
Keep and improve it — it's working but needs better funding and capacity.
Reform it — use vouchers so parents can choose public or private providers.
Wind it down — targeted subsidies for low-income families are enough; the market does the rest.

questions.text: How strictly should Canada regulate large corporations?
questions.subtext: Grocery chains earned record profits during inflation. Banks post billions in quarterly profits. Tech giants pay little tax.

options.text: Aggressive regulation and windfall taxes — corporate power is too concentrated and harms consumers.
Stronger antitrust enforcement and close tax loopholes — fair market, fair taxes.
Current regulations are adequate — targeted enforcement where there are clear abuses.
Deregulate — heavy regulation raises costs and stifles innovation and growth.

questions.text: How should Canada approach gender identity recognition and LGBTQ+ rights?
questions.subtext: Gender-affirming care for youth, self-ID laws, and parental notification policies are active debates nationally.

options.text: Actively expand LGBTQ+ rights — fund gender-affirming care, protect trans youth, end religious exemptions.
Protect existing rights firmly — uphold Charter protections with balanced religious freedom accommodation.
Preserve parental rights — require notification for minors' gender-related decisions at school.
Limit gender ideology in public institutions — children should not be exposed to LGBTQ+ content in schools.

questions.text: What should Canada's policy be on abortion access?
questions.subtext: Canada has no federal restrictions. Late-term access, gestational limits, and parental consent for minors are debated.

options.text: Full, unrestricted access including publicly funded; no gestational limits; no parental consent.
Unrestricted access in first two trimesters; reasonable medical standards after that.
Some restrictions on late-term; parental notification for minors in most cases.
Significant restrictions — life begins at conception; restrict except in specific circumstances.

questions.text: What should determine public school curriculum on social topics?
questions.subtext: Debates over sex-ed, gender identity, Indigenous history, and critical race theory are active in Ontario, Alberta, and BC.

options.text: Inclusive, research-based curriculum — cover LGBTQ+ identities, systemic racism, decolonization comprehensively.
Evidence-based and age-appropriate — include diversity topics but with attention to parental concerns.
Core academics first — values education is the family's job, not the school's.
Protect children from ideological content — parents should have full opt-out rights for any progressive material.

questions.text: How should Canada approach drug use and addiction?
questions.subtext: BC decriminalized small amounts of hard drugs, then partially reversed it. Safe supply programs are expanding. Opioid deaths hit 7,300 in 2022.

options.text: Full decriminalization — addiction is a health issue; criminalization makes it worse and criminalizes the vulnerable.
Harm reduction approach — safe supply, decriminalise personal use, invest in treatment.
Treatment-first, not supply — safe supply normalizes drug use; focus on recovery.
Strict enforcement — drug use is a moral and criminal issue, not a health one.

questions.text: How far should Canada go on Indigenous self-determination?
questions.subtext: UNDRIP implementation is underway. Land rights disputes block pipelines and development. Residential school survivors await reparations.

options.text: Full constitutional recognition of sovereignty — Indigenous nations have jurisdiction over their territories and resources.
Meaningful self-governance within Confederation — strong resource revenue sharing and land rights.
Targeted socioeconomic support — address poverty and health gaps, but equal citizenship within Canadian law.
Move on from reconciliation — decades of apologies and spending without improvement; equal treatment under law.

questions.text: Does systemic racism exist in Canadian institutions, and should government actively address it?
questions.subtext: Data shows racial disparities in policing, incarceration, income, and healthcare. Debates focus on cause and remedy.

options.text: Systemic racism is a defining feature — major policy reform in policing, hiring, and public institutions is urgent.
Racial disparities are real — targeted programs and anti-discrimination enforcement are needed.
Disparities exist but are complex — individual factors matter as much as systemic ones.
Systemic racism is overstated — Canada is one of the most equal societies; focus on individual responsibility.

questions.text: What role should religious values play in Canadian public policy?
questions.subtext: Quebec's Bill 21 bans religious symbols for public servants. Federal debates over religious exemptions from equality law continue.

options.text: Strict separation — religious values have no place in public law or institutions; secularism is non-negotiable.
Accommodate religious freedom but equality law prevails — no exemptions from anti-discrimination.
Religious freedom is a fundamental right — meaningful exemptions in education and social services.
Canada's Christian heritage should be honoured in public life — policy should reflect traditional values.

questions.text: What is the right approach to crime and public safety?
questions.subtext: Violent crime rose 39% from 2015-2022. Debates: root causes vs. enforcement, sentencing reform vs. mandatory minimums.

options.text: Root causes first — poverty, addiction, and inequality drive crime; enforcement without investment fails.
Balance rehabilitation with accountability — smarter policing, treatment, and proportionate sentences.
Stronger enforcement — longer sentences for repeat and violent offenders; police need more resources.
Law and order priority — soft-on-crime policies have failed; mandatory minimums and more police.

questions.text: What is the cause of, and solution to, the gender pay gap?
questions.subtext: Canadian women earn ~$0.89 for every dollar men earn. Pay equity legislation exists but gaps persist.

options.text: Structural discrimination — mandatory pay audits, pay transparency, and strong enforcement are needed.
A real problem — expand pay equity legislation and improve access to childcare.
Partly individual choices — occupational selection explains most of the gap; targeted support is enough.
The gap largely reflects different choices — government intervention risks distorting the market.

questions.text: How strictly should Canada regulate environmental damage from industry?
questions.subtext: Oil sands tailings ponds, pipeline spills, and agricultural runoff are major concerns. Industry says over-regulation costs jobs.

options.text: Polluter pays fully — strict liability, mandatory cleanup, criminal penalties, and no grandfather clauses.
Strong regulations with meaningful enforcement — industry must internalize environmental costs.
Regulations are adequate — focus on technology solutions rather than new rules.
Current regulations are excessive — they kill projects and jobs without meaningful environmental benefit.

questions.text: How much authority should Canadian security agencies have to surveil citizens?
questions.subtext: CSIS, RCMP, and CSE powers expanded post-2001 and post-2014 Parliament attack. Metadata collection, warrant thresholds debated.

options.text: Strict judicial oversight for all surveillance — no bulk collection, strong privacy protections.
Targeted surveillance with warrants — proportionate powers, independent oversight, sunset clauses.
Agencies need operational flexibility — current courts oversight is adequate.
Security agencies should have broad powers — national security threats require proactive surveillance.

questions.text: When civil unrest occurs, what powers should police have?
questions.subtext: The 2022 Convoy, Gaza campus protests (2024), and Indigenous blockades have all tested the limits of police power.

options.text: Police should only use minimum necessary force — rights of assembly and protest are near-absolute.
Balanced approach — lawful protest protected; police can act on genuine public safety threats with court oversight.
Police should restore order firmly — prolonged blockades are not protected protest.
The state must enforce law decisively — weakness emboldens those who disrupt the economy and daily life.

questions.text: How should Canada handle hate speech and online harmful content?
questions.subtext: Bill C-63 proposed a digital safety regulator. Critics call it government censorship. Social media platforms host extremist content.

options.text: Regulate online platforms firmly — harmful content causes real harm; the state must act.
Strengthen enforcement of existing hate speech law — targeted enforcement, not broad speech regulation.
Narrow hate speech laws tightly — the bar for criminality should be very high; err on speech freedom.
Repeal hate speech provisions — free expression is nearly absolute; the state should not regulate speech.

questions.text: Where should Canada set gun ownership rights?
questions.subtext: The 2020 assault-style weapons ban and handgun freeze are under review. Conservatives promise to reverse them.

options.text: Significantly stricter — ban all handguns, restrict long guns, mandatory buyback programs.
Current regulations are appropriate — enforce existing rules more consistently.
Over-regulated — reverse the assault-style ban; focus on criminal enforcement, not lawful owners.
The right to self-defence is fundamental — responsible Canadians should be able to own firearms for protection.

questions.text: Was invocation of the Emergencies Act in February 2022 justified?
questions.subtext: Trudeau invoked the Act to clear the Ottawa convoy blockade. A Federal Court found the invocation unconstitutional in 2024.

options.text: Clearly unjustified — the threshold was not met; the court was right; this was a rights violation.
Borderline — the situation was serious but existing laws would have sufficed.
Justified in context — the economic disruption was severe and other options had failed.
Absolutely justified — the government must be able to act decisively to protect the economy and public order.

questions.text: Should Canada reinstate or expand mandatory minimum sentences for serious crimes?
questions.subtext: The Supreme Court struck down several mandatory minimums as cruel. The Conservative platform proposes reinstating them.

options.text: Abolish mandatory minimums — judicial discretion is essential; mandatory minimums increase incarceration without reducing crime.
Very limited mandatory minimums — only for the most serious violent and sexual offences.
Reinstate mandatory minimums for repeat violent offenders and drug trafficking.
Broad mandatory minimums — judges are too lenient; the public needs predictable harsh sentences.

questions.text: Should Canada expand sharing of citizen data across government agencies to improve security?
questions.subtext: Post-9/11 data sharing between CSIS, CBSA, and CRA has expanded. Foreign interference concerns heighten the debate.

options.text: No — data sharing across agencies without consent is a surveillance state; strict firewalls are essential.
Limited sharing — specific, targeted, with independent oversight and sunset clauses.
Agencies should share data on national security cases — foreign threats require coordination.
Broad integration — the security benefits outweigh privacy concerns; we need a unified threat picture.

questions.text: How much authority should government have over media content?
questions.subtext: Bill C-11 (Online Streaming Act) mandated Canadian content on platforms. Critics call it censorship; supporters call it cultural protection.

options.text: Minimal government control — the state should not regulate content, only fund public broadcasting.
C-11 is reasonable — CanCon requirements help Canadian creators without censoring.
C-11 went too far — repeal it; regulate only for competition, not content.
Stronger content regulation is needed — platforms carry harmful content and must be held accountable.

questions.text: How should Canada handle illegal border crossings and refugee claims?
questions.subtext: Irregular crossings surged 2022-2024. Roxham Road was closed. Deportation and safe third-country debates are ongoing.

options.text: Humane processing — all irregular arrivals deserve full refugee hearings; detention is a last resort.
Process claims fairly but efficiently — faster hearings, clear criteria, deportation for failed claimants.
Firm enforcement — irregular crossings are illegal; detain and deport failed claimants quickly.
Zero tolerance — irregular crossings must be stopped immediately; turn back at the border.

questions.text: Should Canada change the first-past-the-post voting system?
questions.subtext: Trudeau promised electoral reform and broke that promise. Minority governments are now common. The Senate is still unelected.

options.text: Yes — proportional representation would make Parliament actually reflect how Canadians vote.
A mixed system — mixed-member proportional as a compromise between stability and fairness.
FPTP is fine — stable majority governments are more valuable than proportional complexity.
Change would destabilize democracy — the existing system produced 157 years of stable government.

questions.text: What immigration intake is right for Canada?
questions.subtext: Canada hit 1.2M+ newcomers in 2023-24 (all categories). The Carney government has cut targets back to ~395K for 2025.

options.text: High immigration — Canada needs people; diversity is an economic and cultural strength.
Moderate intake at roughly 400K/year — match housing and integration capacity.
Significant reduction — current levels strain infrastructure; prioritize integration first.
Dramatically reduce — open-borders globalism has failed; Canada for Canadians first.

questions.text: How should Canada approach free trade agreements?
questions.subtext: USMCA is under pressure from Trump 2.0 tariffs. Canada relies on the US for 75% of exports. New trade diversification sought.

options.text: Pursue trade with strong labour, environmental, and human rights standards — 'fair trade' not 'free trade'.
Continue free trade but diversify from US dependence — Asia, EU, and Indo-Pacific.
Protect strategic sectors — supply management, culture, and key manufacturing from trade deals.
Protectionism first — Canadian industries and workers should be shielded from foreign competition.

questions.text: How should Canada respond to US pressure — tariffs, territorial threats, and political interference?
questions.subtext: Trump's 51st state rhetoric, 25% tariff threats, and pressure on NORAD and energy are live issues in 2025.

options.text: Diplomatic resolution — Canada and the US are partners; we should negotiate, not retaliate.
Firm but measured response — counter-tariffs on strategic goods, diversify, and stand on international law.
Assert sovereignty strongly — Canada will not be bullied; retaliatory tariffs and alternative alliances.
Canada First — we need economic independence; reduce reliance on the US even if costly.

questions.text: Should Canada's official multiculturalism policy continue unchanged?
questions.subtext: Canada's official multiculturalism has existed since 1971. Immigration skeptics argue it prevents integration; others call it Canada's strength.

options.text: Strengthen multiculturalism — Canada's diversity is its identity; more funding for cultural communities.
Keep multiculturalism but emphasize Canadian civic values and integration outcomes.
Reform multiculturalism — integration into Canadian society should come before cultural preservation.
End multiculturalism policy — it fragments social cohesion; Canada needs one shared identity.

questions.text: How much should Canada spend on foreign aid and international development?
questions.subtext: Canada spends ~0.3% of GNI on foreign aid, well below the UN's 0.7% target. The Conservative platform proposes cuts.

options.text: Increase to meet the 0.7% UN target — global poverty and instability affect Canada too.
Maintain current levels and focus aid strategically on Canadian foreign policy objectives.
Reduce — foreign aid is a luxury when Canadians face housing and healthcare crises.
Cut foreign aid dramatically — charity begins at home; the UN target is an outdated guilt payment.

questions.text: Should Canada meet the NATO 2% GDP defence spending target?
questions.subtext: Canada spends ~1.37% of GDP on defence, well below NATO's 2% target. The war in Ukraine and Arctic security are pressures.

options.text: Prioritize diplomacy over military spending — Canada's foreign policy strength is peacekeeping, not arms.
Meet the 2% gradually — but invest in peacekeeping capacity and multilateral institutions too.
Meet the 2% now — Canada is a free-rider and we must pull our weight to maintain US alliance.
Rearm significantly beyond 2% — the world is more dangerous; Canada must be able to defend itself.

questions.text: How many refugees should Canada accept, and under what conditions?
questions.subtext: Canada accepted ~40,000 government-assisted refugees in 2023. The UN HCR says global displacement is at record highs.

options.text: Significantly increase — Canada has the capacity and moral obligation to take far more refugees.
Maintain current levels — Canada does its fair share relative to population.
Reduce targets — integration resources are stretched; prioritize economic immigrants.
Minimal refugees — Canada's obligations are fulfilled; domestic need must come first.

questions.text: How should the federal government respond to Quebec's Bill 96 (French language law)?
questions.subtext: Bill 96 restricts English and imposes French across most areas of Quebec life, affecting Anglophones and newcomers.

options.text: Respect Quebec's right to protect French — linguistic minorities everywhere deserve support.
Bilingualism is the framework — federal government should protect both official language minorities.
Provinces should decide — Ottawa should stay out of provincial language policy.
French-Canada's survival justifies strong measures — Quebec's cultural nationalism is legitimate.

questions.text: How should Canada assert sovereignty over the Arctic?
questions.subtext: Russia, the US, Denmark, and China are all active in Canada's claimed Arctic. Climate change is opening new shipping routes.

options.text: Multilateral Arctic governance — shared sovereignty through international law and Arctic Council.
Maintain current international engagement while investing in surveillance and Indigenous partnerships.
Assert sovereignty firmly — Canadian armed presence, infrastructure, and clear territorial claims.
Militarize the Arctic — Canada must be able to defend it unilaterally against Russia and China.

questions.text: Should Canada use policy to protect Canadian culture from American media dominance?
questions.subtext: Streaming platforms now exceed traditional TV. C-11 mandates Canadian content. Critics say this distorts market choices.

options.text: Strong cultural protection — Canadian stories, voices, and creators need government support to survive.
Moderate CanCon requirements — support Canadian creators but don't mandate what audiences watch.
Minimal intervention — audiences should choose; subsidize public broadcasters only.
No cultural protectionism — Canadian culture is strong enough to compete; protectionism is paternalism.

questions.text: How should Canada position itself on the Israel-Gaza conflict?
questions.subtext: Canada abstained on UN resolutions in 2023-24, then shifted. Arms sales, humanitarian aid, and recognition of Palestinian state are active debates.

options.text: Strong support for Palestinian statehood — halt arms sales to Israel; increase humanitarian aid; back ICJ proceedings.
Two-state solution with balanced critique — condemn civilian casualties on both sides; support ceasefire.
Support Israel's right to self-defence — Hamas is a terrorist organization; civilian casualties are Israel's regret, not intent.
Strong support for Israel — the only democracy in the region; Canada should not criticize its closest allies.

questions.text: How should Canada balance decarbonization with fossil fuel employment?
questions.subtext: Over 200,000 Canadians work in oil and gas. Net-zero 2050 is government policy. The just transition is debated.

options.text: Rapid green transition — fund workers through retraining and income support; phase out oil sands by 2040.
Manage the transition — invest in clean energy while supporting oil sector workers through gradual phase-down.
Oil is essential for decades — Canada should maximize extraction and export while developing clean tech.
No forced transition — abandon net-zero targets; oil is Canada's economic lifeblood.

questions.text: How seriously should Canada take foreign interference from China, India, and others?
questions.subtext: The Hogue Commission found evidence of Chinese and Indian interference in federal elections. Several MPs are implicated.

options.text: Treat it as a national security crisis — public registry of foreign agents, full expulsion of foreign influence operations.
Take it seriously with proportionate response — strengthen CSIS, prosecute violators, demand accountability from implicated MPs.
It's real but overstated — don't damage diplomatic relationships with evidence-free accusations.
It's a distraction — domestic poverty and healthcare matter more than geopolitical conspiracy theories.

questions.text: How do you assess the 2022 Ottawa Freedom Convoy and its legacy?
questions.subtext: The Convoy occupied Ottawa for 3 weeks, blockaded border crossings, and prompted the Emergencies Act. It remains polarizing.

options.text: A legitimate protest movement — vaccine mandates were overreach; the protest, if not all tactics, was justified.
Mixed — legitimate grievances about mandates, but the blockades crossed a legal and democratic line.
Largely unjustified — pandemic measures were warranted; the convoy caused economic harm and threatened democracy.
An insurrectionist movement — it was funded by foreign actors and sought to overturn democratic government.

questions.text: Should Canada maintain supply management in dairy, eggs, and poultry?
questions.subtext: Supply management raises food prices 8-30% but supports ~14,000 farm families. Trade partners demand it be opened.

options.text: Keep it — farmers deserve income stability; not everything should be at the mercy of global commodity markets.
Retain with reforms — allow gradual market adjustment; provide transition support.
Phase it out — Canadians pay too much for dairy and eggs; trade access matters more.
Abolish supply management — it's a cartel that punishes consumers to enrich a small number of farm families.

questions.text: How should Canada regulate big tech companies collecting and monetizing Canadian data?
questions.subtext: Bill C-27 (Digital Charter) stalled. Google, Meta, and Amazon collect extensive data on Canadians with limited accountability.

options.text: Mandatory data rights — Canadians own their data; opt-in consent, right to deletion, and strong fines.
Comprehensive regulation — GDPR-style framework with real enforcement and penalties.
Industry self-regulation with minimum standards — over-regulation will drive investment away.
No regulation needed — competition and market forces will protect consumers.

questions.text: What should Canada's approach to China be?
questions.subtext: China is Canada's second-largest trading partner. It has detained Canadians, interfered in elections, and threatened Taiwan.

options.text: Decouple strategically — reduce economic dependence; stand firm on human rights and Taiwan.
Competitive coexistence — engage on trade, but push back on interference and human rights.
Pragmatic engagement — trade and diplomatic relationships are too valuable to jeopardize.
Full integration into international institutions — confront China through multilateral, not unilateral, means.

questions.text: What should the federal government do about poverty in Canada?
questions.subtext: Canada's official poverty rate is 7.4% (MBM). 1 in 5 children live in food insecurity. The Senate passed anti-poverty measures that stalled.

options.text: Poverty is a policy failure — a guaranteed livable income and major expansion of social programs is needed now.
Significant expansion of targeted supports — housing, childcare, dental, pharmacare as pillars.
Current programs are enough — improve delivery and target better; more spending isn't the answer.
Social safety net dependency is itself a cause of poverty — work requirements and reduction of benefits.

questions.text: What is the best solution to Canada's healthcare wait time problem?
questions.subtext: Canada ranks last in OECD for wait times. Average wait from GP to specialist to treatment: 27+ weeks.

options.text: Massive public investment — more doctors, nurses, hospitals; train domestically and streamline foreign credentials.
Federal-provincial investment surge — tied transfers to accountability on wait times.
Allow private surgery centres — dequeue the public system by letting those who can pay, go private.
Full parallel private system — competition will reduce waits for everyone through innovation.

questions.text: How should Canada address the cost and accessibility of post-secondary education?
questions.subtext: Average student debt is $28,000. Tuition has risen 40% in real terms since 2000. International student caps are new policy.

options.text: Free university — post-secondary should be treated like K-12; an educated population is a public good.
Significant grants and low-interest loans; freeze tuition; write off first $10K of debt.
Income-contingent repayment — debt paid back only when earning above a threshold; no blanket forgiveness.
Education is an investment — students should pay full freight; scholarships for the talented.

questions.text: Should Canada implement universal pharmacare?
questions.subtext: The NDP-Liberal pharmacare deal (2024) covers diabetes and contraception. Full pharmacare could cost $15B/year.

options.text: Full universal pharmacare now — drugs are part of healthcare; no Canadian should choose between food and medicine.
Expand gradually — start with catastrophic drug coverage; build toward full pharmacare.
Employer plans work fine for most — target pharmacare only at those without coverage.
Pharmacare is unaffordable — the private system works; government shouldn't displace it.

questions.text: How should Canada fund and deliver long-term care for seniors?
questions.subtext: The COVID-19 pandemic killed 14,000+ long-term care residents. Most LTC is privately operated. Wait times for public beds: 4+ years.

options.text: Full public not-for-profit LTC — profit motive in elder care is incompatible with dignity.
National standards with increased public funding — hold private operators to strict accountability.
Private operators are fine if properly regulated — the issue is enforcement, not ownership.
Market will allocate best — let families choose; government-run LTC has been the worst performer.

questions.text: How should Ottawa respond to Alberta's sovereignty movement and firewall rhetoric?
questions.subtext: The Alberta Sovereignty Act (2023) claims the right to refuse federal laws. Separation polling hits 30%+.

options.text: Negotiate earnestly — alienation is real; asymmetric federalism and resource revenue sharing must be addressed.
Respect jurisdiction but enforce the Constitution — provinces cannot nullify federal law.
Stand firm — appeasement of separatism strengthens it; enforce the Constitution without negotiation.
Alberta's grievances are manufactured — push back hard against firewall politics.

questions.text: Is Canada's level of wealth inequality a problem that government should actively address?
questions.subtext: Canada's top 1% hold 25% of total wealth. The Gini coefficient has risen. Billionaire wealth doubled during COVID.

options.text: A defining crisis — wealth concentration threatens democracy; wealth taxes, inheritance taxes, and redistribution.
A significant problem — capital gains inclusion, estate taxes, and closing corporate loopholes.
Inequality exists but economic growth lifts all boats — focus on opportunity, not redistribution.
Wealth inequality is the natural result of talent and effort — redistribution punishes success.

questions.text: What should Canada's response to the TRC Calls to Action look like?
questions.subtext: Of 94 Calls to Action from the 2015 Truth and Reconciliation Commission, fewer than half are complete a decade later.

options.text: Full implementation is a legal and moral obligation — it must be funded and completed urgently.
Prioritize the most impactful calls — child welfare, health, language, and education.
Much has been done — reconciliation requires partnership, not just government checklists.
Canada has moved on — ongoing focus on past wrongs prevents building a shared future.

questions.text: Should Canada adopt proportional representation for federal elections?
questions.subtext: Every government since 1980 has won a majority with under 40% of votes. The 2021 election cost $600M for a near-identical Parliament.

options.text: Yes — FPTP is a democratic deficit; proportional representation is more fair and produces better policy.
A mixed system — retain local representation with proportional top-up.
FPTP produces stable government — proportional systems create endless coalitions.
The existing system has served Canada well — change would be destabilizing.

questions.text: Was Canada right to complete the Trans Mountain pipeline expansion?
questions.subtext: Trans Mountain cost taxpayers $34B after the government bought it for $4.5B in 2018. Oil carries 890,000 barrels/day.

options.text: Wrong — public money should not fund fossil fuel infrastructure; it undermines climate commitments.
Complicated — the resource is real; the government intervention prevented foreign ownership.
Right — Canada needs to get oil to tidewater; Asian markets reduce US dependence.
Absolutely right — energy sovereignty; oil sands are Canada's greatest asset.

questions.text: Should the Bank of Canada be more responsive to government direction on interest rates?
questions.subtext: High interest rates (2022-2024) slowed inflation but crushed mortgage holders. Some argue the Bank is too independent.

options.text: Fully independent — monetary policy must be insulated from political pressure.
Maintain independence but improve accountability and transparency to Parliament.
Government should be able to issue directives in exceptional circumstances.
The Bank should serve elected government — technocrats should not control the economy.

questions.text: Should the Canadian government regulate social media to protect youth?
questions.subtext: Rates of adolescent anxiety and depression have risen sharply since 2012, correlating with smartphone adoption.

options.text: Age verification requirements and content restrictions are necessary — the harm to youth is documented.
Require platforms to implement parental controls and disclose algorithmic amplification.
Educate parents and youth — regulation of content risks setting precedents for speech control.
Not the government's role — parents should monitor their children's screen use.

questions.text: Should Canada adopt a more assertive, interventionist foreign policy to promote democracy and security?
questions.subtext: Scoop Jackson Democrats and neoconservatives argued that liberal democracy must be actively promoted and defended — by force if necessary. Should Canada be a more active force in the world?

options.text: Peacekeeping and multilateralism — Canada's role is to mediate and support international institutions, not project power.
Selective engagement — support democracies and allies but avoid unilateral military action.
Canada should be a more capable military power — NATO burden-sharing and democracy promotion require real capacity.
Active strategic power — Canada should use economic and military tools to advance democratic values and deter authoritarian states.

questions.text: Is the rules-based international order — the UN, WTO, ICC — worth defending?
questions.subtext: The post-WWII institutions are under pressure from China, Russia, the US under Trump, and rising populism. Are they still worth defending?

options.text: Absolutely — the rules-based order, despite its flaws, is the architecture that prevents great power war.
Reform it, don't abandon it — make the UN Security Council and WTO more representative.
Pragmatic — support international institutions where they advance Canadian interests, bypass them where they don't.
It's a relic — China and Russia hollowed it out; Canada should operate through direct alliances, not paper institutions.
